Artificial Intelligence

Data Engineering

Technology

Programming

Data Science

8 min read
Just now

--

A data engineer’s honest take on why most AI projects quietly die — and what’s actually missing.

I work as a data engineer. For most of my career, nobody outside my team really cared what I did.
I built pipelines. Moved data from one place to another. Fixed things when they broke at 2 AM. And stayed invisible. Data engineering was the plumbing of the tech world. Nobody talks about plumbing until the water stops.
Then AI happened. And suddenly, everyone is talking about the plumbing.
Here is the thing most people don’t know. Over the last couple of years, companies everywhere rushed to add AI to their products. Chatbots, copilots, smart assistants — you name it. Most of these projects quietly failed or got stuck. And when teams sat down to figure out why, the answer was almost never the AI model itself.
The models are good. Really good.
The problem was the data underneath.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Chef and the Spoiled Ingredients
Think of an AI model like a world-class chef.
You can hire the best chef on the planet. Give them a state-of-the-art kitchen, the finest equipment, a Michelin star pedigree. But if you hand them spoiled vegetables and expired milk — dinner is going to be terrible. The chef isn’t the problem. The ingredients are.
That’s exactly what’s happening inside companies right now.
They pay for powerful AI models, plug them into their internal data, and expect magic. But that internal data is often a mess. It’s outdated. It’s duplicated across five different systems that don’t agree with each other. Half of it has no labels explaining what it even means.
And when the AI reads all of that and confidently gives a wrong answer, everyone blames the AI.
I’ve seen this up close. A dashboard breaks because someone changed a column name upstream and didn’t tell anyone. Numbers stop matching between two reports. A table that was supposed to update daily silently stopped updating three weeks ago — and nobody noticed. These things were annoying before AI. Embarrassing, sure. But a human would usually catch them before real damage happened.
Now imagine a customer-facing chatbot sitting on top of that same broken data, answering questions all day with full confidence. It will quote a price that changed last month. It will tell a customer about a policy that no longer exists.
There are already public cases of exactly this happening — companies honoring wrong prices and issuing public apologies because their AI was confidently reading bad data.
That’s not an AI problem. That’s a data problem wearing an AI costume.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

What “AI-Ready Data” Actually Means
This is why the phrase AI-ready data has quietly become one of the hottest topics in tech right now. It sounds like corporate jargon, but the idea behind it is dead simple.
Before you point an AI at your data, that data needs to pass a few basic tests. Let me break each one down in plain English.

1. Is It Fresh?
AI doesn’t know your sales table stopped syncing last Tuesday. It reads whatever is there and answers based on that. If the data is stale, every answer is stale. Old data plus a confident AI equals confident nonsense — delivered politely and at scale.

2. Is It Clean and Consistent?
If a customer’s name is spelled three different ways across three systems, the AI treats all three as separate truths. If the same order appears twice in a database, the AI counts it twice. Humans notice duplicates and quietly shrug. Machines don’t shrug. They just calculate.

3. Does It Have Meaning Attached?
This one is underrated and almost never talked about outside data circles.
A table full of numbers means absolutely nothing without context. What does the “status” column actually mean? Is 1 active or inactive? Which revenue number is the official one — the finance team’s version or the sales team’s version? Humans carry this knowledge in their heads. AI needs it written down. This is what data engineers call metadata, definitions, and lineage — knowing what the data means, where it came from, and what happened to it along the way.

4. Can the AI Actually Find It?
Most modern AI tools use something called RAG — short for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Ignore the scary name. It just means the AI searches your data first, then uses what it finds to answer your question. Think of it like giving the AI a search engine over your own company’s information before it responds.
The problem is: if your data is scattered, badly organized, or locked inside formats the search can’t read — the AI finds the wrong things. Or nothing at all.
Building these search pipelines is now a core part of my job. And honestly? It’s just the same old data pipeline work with a few new steps. The engineering hasn’t changed that much. The stakes have.

5. Is It Safe to Show?
This is the part that keeps companies up at night. An AI connected to company data can accidentally show salary information to the wrong employee. It can surface private customer details in a response to someone who shouldn’t see them.
Someone has to build the guardrails — the rules that decide who the AI is allowed to see data for, and what stays locked away. Guess whose job that is.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

6. What About All the Messy Stuff?
Here’s the big one nobody talks about enough.
Around 80% of company data isn’t sitting in neat tables. It lives in PDFs, emails, contracts, meeting notes, scanned documents, old slide decks, support tickets. Classic data engineering mostly ignored this pile. We focused on databases, warehouses, structured tables.
AI wants all of it. Every messy document, every scanned form. Turning that mountain of unstructured chaos into something an AI can actually use is probably the single biggest new challenge in the field right now — and most companies haven’t even started.

The Quiet Promotion Nobody Announced
Here’s what I find genuinely funny about all of this. Data engineers didn’t change. The world changed around us.
Five years ago, my job was to keep dashboards running. If a pipeline broke, a report looked wrong and someone complained on Monday morning. Annoying, but survivable. The blast radius was small.
Today, if my pipeline breaks, an AI product gives wrong answers to real customers, in real time, continuously, until someone catches it. The cost of my mistakes went up massively. And so did the value of my work.
The job itself is shifting too. It’s no longer just “move data from A to B.” It now includes:

• Building pipelines that feed AI search systems

• Preparing datasets used to test whether the AI is answering correctly

• Setting up monitoring that catches bad data before it poisons a model

• Writing formal agreements between teams — called data contracts — so nobody silently changes a column and breaks everything downstream without warning
The line between data engineering and AI engineering is getting blurry. And the people who can stand comfortably on both sides of that line are in serious, serious demand right now.
Companies figured this out the hard way, mostly by failing first. The winners in AI won’t be the ones with the fanciest model. Everyone can rent the same models now — GPT, Claude, Gemini, all available with a credit card. The winners will be the ones whose data is clean, fresh, well-organized, and trusted.
That’s not a model advantage. That’s a data engineering advantage.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Five Questions to Ask Before You Trust AI With Your Data
If your company is building anything with AI — or you’re trying to figure out whether the hype at your workplace is real — ask these five questions. You don’t need to be technical to ask them. You just need to be in the room.
1. How do we know our data is up to date, and would we even notice if it stopped updating?
2. If two systems show different numbers for the same thing, which one wins — and is that decision written down anywhere?
3. Can the AI accidentally see data it shouldn’t, like HR records or private customer information?
4. What happens to all our documents and PDFs — or is the AI only looking at nice, clean tables?
5. When the AI gives a wrong answer, can we actually trace back and find out why?
If nobody in the room can answer these confidently, the AI project is standing on sand. It might look impressive in a demo. It will not survive real users.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Unglamorous Truth
AI is the most exciting thing to happen to tech in decades. I get why all the attention goes to the models. They talk, they write, they feel like science fiction made real.
But here’s the quiet truth behind every AI product that actually works: somebody did boring, careful, unglamorous data work before any of that was possible.
Nobody claps for the plumbing. Nobody writes headlines about data pipelines. The engineer who spent three weeks cleaning and organizing a dataset before the AI was even turned on — they don’t get a mention in the press release.
But every impressive AI product you’ve used recently works because some data engineer, somewhere, made sure the water was clean before it reached the tap.
The models get the headlines. The data decides who actually wins.

I’m a data engineer writing about the real, unglamorous side of AI and data systems. If this was useful, follow me for more — no hype, just the stuff that actually matters.
